Summary: FOXR2 activation defines the molecular entity "CNS neuroblastoma with FOXR2 activation" (CNS NB-FOXR2) among CNS tumors. CNS NB-FOXR2 consists of a relatively pure population of CNS-PNET and is enriched for variants termed CNS neuroblastoma and ganglioneuroblastoma. Identification of CNS NB-FOXR2 clarifies that histopathological CNS-PNET variants resolve into two primary entities, ETMR and CNS NB-FOXR2. CNS NB-FOXR2 tumors displayed embryonal architecture and a small-cell phenotype. CNS NB-FOXR2 tumors commonly showed neuronal differentiation and histologic features such as neuropil, neurocytic or ganglion cells, perivascular anuclear zones, nuclear palisades, and Homer Wright rosettes. Tumors in this group nearly uniformly expressed OLIG2 and synaptophysin. In CNS NB-FOXR2 tumors FOXR2 is activated and overexpressed relative to other CNS tumor entities and normal brain. Complex inter- and intrachromosomal rearrangements converged on FOXR2 in multiple sequenced samples and produced fusion transcripts retaining FOXR2 coding sequence fused to upstream noncoding exons or other structural alterations. These events indicate FOXR2 activation by promoters of active genes and consequent oncogenic activity.

Summary: FOXR2 is essential in neuroblastoma. It defines an activated subset and is an independent prognostic factor associated with unfavorable clinical outcome. In neuroblastoma, FOXR2 stabilizes MYCN protein, indicating an alternative mechanism to MYCN amplification. MYCN stabilization by FOXR2 may explain the poor clinical outcome and provides crucial knowledge for targeted treatment of FOXR2‑activated neuroblastoma. The mechanism of FOXR2 activation in peripheral neuroblastoma remains elusive because gene fusions driving FOXR2 expression were rarely detected, in contrast to CNS neuroblastoma. Poor survival of the FOXR2 group was observed in the NB97 and NB2004 RNA‑seq studies, and similar responses to different induction or consolidation treatment arms make protocol differences an unlikely explanation. Nearly identical 5‑ and 10‑year event‑free survival and progression‑free survival for FOXR2‑expressing neuroblastoma indicate that primarily early events occur in this group.
Reference: Schmitt-Hoffner, F., van Rijn, S., Toprak, U. H., Mauermann, M., Rosemann, F., Heit-Mondrzyk, A., Hübner, J. M., Camgöz, A., Hartlieb, S., Pfister, S. M., Henrich, K. O., Westermann, F., & Kool, M. (2021). FOXR2 Stabilizes MYCN Protein and Identifies Non-MYCN-Amplified Neuroblastoma Patients With Unfavorable Outcome. J Clin Oncol, 39(29), 3217-3228. https://doi.org/10.1200/jco.20.02540

Summary: The passages describe systematic analyses (bulk and single-cell transcriptomics, reference assembly, and mapping) used to define FOXR2's role. NB-FOXR2 tumors originate from LHX6+/DLX+ interneuron lineages derived from the medial ganglionic eminence in the ventral telencephalon. Prenatal Foxr2 targeting to the ganglionic eminences in mice induced postnatal cortical tumors recapitulating human NB-FOXR2–specific molecular signatures. FOXR2 chromatin profiling associated FOXR2 with ETS transcriptional networks and showed direct FOXR2 binding at genes encoding transcription factors that coordinate initiation of gliogenesis. The data indicate ventral telencephalon–derived LHX6+/DLX+ interneurons are susceptible to FOXR2-driven oncogenesis and FOXR2-induced activation of glial programs may explain the tumors' mixed neuronal and oligodendroglial features. NB-FOXR2 tumors express a transcription factor fingerprint unique to medial ganglionic eminence–derived neuron lineages and absent in other FOXR2+ brain tumors and in a FOXR2-transduced cell line, and the observations indicate the fingerprint is not likely a consequence of FOXR2 activation and instead may be maintained from the tumor lineage of origin.

D., Bandopadhayay, P., Pathania, M., Jabado, N., & Kleinman, C. L. (2025). FOXR2 Targets LHX6+/DLX+ Neural Lineages to Drive Central Nervous System Neuroblastoma. Cancer Res, 85(2), 231-250. https://doi.org/10.1158/0008-5472.can-24-2248

Summary: The passages state that FOXR2 activation is regarded as pathognomonic for CNS neuroblastoma (NB). FOXR2 overexpression was observed among embryonal tumors and included 11 CNS NB-FOXR2 cases, six pineoblastoma cases (five PB-FOXR2 and one pineal anlage tumor), and one medulloblastoma. Except for a single SHH-activated, TP53-mutant medulloblastoma, the embryonal tumors with FOXR2 overexpression, including the CNS NB-FOXR2 cases, did not harbor other pathogenic or likely pathogenic genetic alterations. All CNS NB-FOXR2 were cerebral cortical tumors. Other CNS tumors with FOXR2 overexpression show regional predilections depending on the specific tumor type. The results support that CNS tumors with FOXR2 overexpression exhibit diverse histopathologic, molecular, and clinical features.
Reference: Siskar, A. N., Hanzlik, E., Cardenas, M. F., Eldomery, M. K., Pinto, S., Tinkle, C. L., Zhang, Q., Li, X., Lin, T., Dhanda, S. Summary: CNS Neuroblastoma with FOXR2 activation (CNS NB-FOXR2) was identified as one of four tumor groups discovered among previously diagnosed CNS‑PNETs.
